title: Cleft ash spade handle
subtitle: A replacement spade handle shaped from a green ash billet on the shaving horse
excerpt: A broken spade handle is replaced by riving a straight-grained ash billet and drawknifing it to the D-handle form on the shaving horse. Riven ash has grain running the full length of the handle, far stronger than a sawn replacement. The project takes half a day.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the cleft ash handle shaping method; Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry for the socket taper and fitting sequence.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently riven wood, still high in moisture content. Green ash shapes readily on the shaving horse and dries straight in a tool handle.
Split along the grain with a froe and maul. Riven ash handles have continuous grain from socket to grip, far stronger than sawn stock.
A two-handled blade pulled toward the body along the workpiece. The main shaping tool for handle blanks at the shaving horse.
A foot-pedalled bench that clamps the workpiece for two-handed drawknife and spokeshave work.

content.text: A replacement spade handle from a hardware shop is almost always sawn from a board, with grain running diagonally or across the section. Under the lever stress of digging, a cross-grain section can snap. A
riven
ash handle has grain running from socket to grip with no interruption; it flexes under load rather than snapping.
Green
ash rives cleanly and shapes quickly at the
shaving horse
. The handle is fitted to the spade socket while still slightly green; the drying wood tightens the socket fit.
Wood

Ash is the standard spade-handle wood: tough, flexible in the long-grain direction, and available from coppice or hedgerow throughout Britain. The billet should be 700 to 800 mm long, 60 to 70 mm in diameter, and as straight-grained as possible. Avoid billets with visible knots in the lower half of the length, where the socket taper will sit. Coppice poles of the right diameter rive cleanly and have the straightest grain.
Tools
Froe and beetle: for riving the billet into a handle blank.
Drawknife and shaving horse: for removing waste and shaping the shaft.
Spokeshave, flat: for final rounding and blending the socket taper.
Bow saw: for crosscutting the blank to length.
Leather strop.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
Riving the blank

Stand the billet on its end on a chopping block. Set the froe across the end-grain on the diameter line and drive it with the beetle. Lever the froe handle to split the billet. One half is the handle blank; the other can produce a second handle. The blank at this stage is a rough half-round section 60 to 70 mm across and 700 mm long.
Shaping the shaft
Clamp the blank in the
shaving horse
. Use the
drawknife

to remove the rough face from the split side, bringing the blank to an octagonal section. Then shave to round, working toward the target shaft diameter of 28 to 32 mm. Keep drawknife passes long and consistent, reading the grain direction as you go. Work from each end toward the midpoint where grain direction reverses.
The socket taper

The bottom 80 to 100 mm of the handle tapers to fit the spade socket. Measure the socket diameter at the opening: most garden spade sockets are 28 to 34 mm. The taper should be a gentle cone that seats 60 mm into the socket. Shape the taper with the drawknife first, then refine with the spokeshave for a smooth, even taper. Test-fit frequently; the handle should seat firmly in the socket under light mallet taps and not bottom out before the shoulder seats.
Fitting and drying

Drive the handle home with the beetle. Bore a 5 mm hole through the socket collar and handle and drive a short steel rivet or a tight wooden peg to lock the handle in the socket. The handle will shrink slightly across the grain over the following two to four weeks, which tightens the fit further. After drying, the socket should show no movement.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: The socket taper bottomed out before seating fully
attrs.cause: Taper too steep at the tip
attrs.fix: Remove the handle and reduce the lower 30 mm of taper by another 1 to 2 mm across diameter. Re-test. Do not attempt to shorten the socket itself.

attrs.symptom: The handle wobbles in the socket after drying
attrs.cause: Taper too loose or the locking peg did not seat properly
attrs.fix: Remove the peg, wrap the taper section with a single layer of linen thread soaked in raw linseed oil, and re-drive. The thread takes up the gap and dries rigid. Re-bore and re-pin.
